story:
Twenty-five years ago at Jennifer’s birthday party, I met Sandi. I wasn’t looking—my breakup with Tania was fresh, and therapy had turned me into an “open book with frayed pages.” Then a redhead with a radiant smile barreled into me, spilled my wine, apologized, and ran to the restroom. When she returned with towels and a replacement glass, muttering at herself with disarming humor, I was gone. So was my self-pity.

She was Sandi Statler—Harvard and Columbia trained psychoanalyst, witty, grounded, magnetic. Within weeks we were an item. A year later, we married. Jonas and Serena arrived two years apart. Life felt like a recurring dream: shared values, easy conversation, hot chemistry, real partnership.

Then it didn’t. Her practice took off, conference invitations multiplied, and we drifted. The kids left for college. Even when we were together, Sandi seemed far away. The sex went from hot to not. I suggested couples therapy. She suggested her own therapist and office suite-mate, Brett Bandler. I balked—conflict of interest seemed obvious—but she swore he was the best and would be objective. Against my instincts, I agreed.

Brett’s office felt wrong: bright, sparse, a “couch” that read more like a bed, erotic-leaning art, and his opener—“I feel I know you”—because Sandi had spoken about me for years. Bias much? Still, I stayed. Sessions were reasonable, measured. For a while, things improved. We went on dates. Sandi seemed present. I let my guard down.

Then I surprised her at lunchtime with sushi and flowers and met her stepping out of Brett’s office, disheveled. She blanched, said she’d had a painful epiphany in therapy. I wanted to believe. But back home, something was off. Attempts at intimacy fizzled; I felt like an unwelcome suitor. I buffed up at the gym, dyed my hair, pierced an ear—more cringe than cure. Sandi even warned friends not to “encourage” the new me.

I took a quick archaeology dig in Egypt to clear my head and came home two days early, ready with a plan to save us. I crept into our dark bedroom to spoon my wife and felt, immediately, that the body beside me wasn’t Sandi’s alone. I flipped on the light and Brett sat up. Sandi, a heavy sleeper, woke seconds later. Both said, “I can explain.” I couldn’t listen. The bed I’d returned to fix everything was the bed where everything broke.

I checked into a hotel. The next morning, Sandi called: contrition, promises, “We can fix this.” I told her I couldn’t unsee what I’d seen. Their relationship was more than a boundary slip; it was a serious ethical violation. I considered reporting them but chose not to drag it through the boards. My lawyer used the leverage; the divorce settlement was favorable. I tried not to be bitter. Still, I collected therapist jokes like artifacts and flinched at red hair.

Two years later, at Jennifer’s fiftieth, my friend introduced me to a raven-haired woman with the same room-brightening pull Sandi once had. I smiled, shook her hand, and left the party. Some ruins you excavate. Some you leave untouched. Some things are unfixable.
